content.text: A release freeze is an operational policy that restricts or prohibits deployments when the error budget has been consumed below a defined threshold. The freeze is not punitive — it is a protection mechanism. When a service has already experienced significant reliability degradation (as evidenced by high budget consumption), each new deployment carries the risk of making things worse. The freeze preserves the remaining budget for natural recovery rather than risking additional consumption.

The freeze is temporary. As old errors age out of the rolling measurement window, the budget recovers automatically. A service that exhausted its budget in week 2 of a 30-day window may have substantial budget recovery by week 4, as the week-2 incidents no longer count against the current window.
A release freeze is not a statement that the team has failed — it is the error budget policy working as designed. The freeze protects users from further degradation while the budget recovers.

attrs.alt: Release freeze policy state machine — budget level drives policy, policy drives allowed deploy types

content.text: The release freeze policy has four levels, each corresponding to a budget threshold. The thresholds are the same thresholds used in the error budget policy defined alongside the SLO. Consistency between the budget policy and the freeze policy is essential — two documents with different thresholds create confusion when the budget is contested.

The 50% threshold for elevated caution is chosen because a service at 50% budget has consumed half its reliability allowance for the window. Feature deploys introduce risk — they are the category most likely to cause an incident. Requiring SRE review at 50% creates a checkpoint without blocking all development.

The 25% threshold for reliability freeze is chosen because at 25% remaining, a single P1 incident could exhaust the remainder. At this level, the risk of a feature deploy causing an SLA breach is real. Only fixes that directly improve reliability (reducing error rate, fixing the root cause of recent incidents) are permitted.

A release freeze policy is meaningless if it is not enforced. Verbal agreements to 'check before deploying' fail under time pressure. The freeze must be enforced in the deployment pipeline — the deploy must fail automatically if the policy level prohibits it.

# deploy_gate.py — CI/CD pipeline integration for release freeze enforcement
import sys
import requests
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class DeployType(Enum):
    FEATURE = "feature"
    BUG_FIX = "bug_fix"
    RELIABILITY = "reliability"
    EXPERIMENT = "experiment"
    EMERGENCY = "emergency"

class PolicyLevel(Enum):
    NORMAL = "normal"
    ELEVATED_CAUTION = "elevated_caution"
    RELIABILITY_FREEZE = "reliability_freeze"
    EXHAUSTED = "exhausted"

POLICY_MATRIX = {
    PolicyLevel.NORMAL: {
        DeployType.FEATURE: "allow",
        DeployType.BUG_FIX: "allow",
        DeployType.RELIABILITY: "allow",
        DeployType.EXPERIMENT: "allow",
        DeployType.EMERGENCY: "allow",
    },
    PolicyLevel.ELEVATED_CAUTION: {
        DeployType.FEATURE: "review",       # requires SRE approval
        DeployType.BUG_FIX: "allow",
        DeployType.RELIABILITY: "allow",
        DeployType.EXPERIMENT: "block",
        DeployType.EMERGENCY: "allow",
    },
    PolicyLevel.RELIABILITY_FREEZE: {
        DeployType.FEATURE: "block",
        DeployType.BUG_FIX: "review",       # requires VP approval
        DeployType.RELIABILITY: "allow",
        DeployType.EXPERIMENT: "block",
        DeployType.EMERGENCY: "review",     # requires VP approval
    },
    PolicyLevel.EXHAUSTED: {
        DeployType.FEATURE: "block",
        DeployType.BUG_FIX: "block",
        DeployType.RELIABILITY: "review",   # requires CTO approval
        DeployType.EXPERIMENT: "block",
        DeployType.EMERGENCY: "review",     # requires CTO approval
    },
}

def get_current_policy_level(prometheus_url: str, service: str, slo: float = 0.999) -> PolicyLevel:
    budget_remaining = get_budget_remaining(prometheus_url, service, slo)
    if budget_remaining > 0.5:
        return PolicyLevel.NORMAL
    if budget_remaining > 0.25:
        return PolicyLevel.ELEVATED_CAUTION
    if budget_remaining > 0.0:
        return PolicyLevel.RELIABILITY_FREEZE
    return PolicyLevel.EXHAUSTED

def check_deploy_allowed(
    prometheus_url: str,
    service: str,
    deploy_type: DeployType,
    approval_token: str | None = None,
) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    level = get_current_policy_level(prometheus_url, service)
    decision = POLICY_MATRIX[level][deploy_type]

    if decision == "allow":
        return True, f"Deploy allowed: {level.value} policy permits {deploy_type.value}"

    if decision == "block":
        return False, (
            f"Deploy blocked: {level.value} policy prohibits {deploy_type.value} deploys. "
            f"Only reliability work is permitted until budget recovers."
        )

    if decision == "review":
        if approval_token and is_valid_approval(approval_token, level, deploy_type):
            return True, f"Deploy allowed: approved override for {level.value} policy"
        return False, (
            f"Deploy blocked: {level.value} policy requires approval for {deploy_type.value}. "
            f"Obtain approval and re-run with --approval-token."
        )

    return False, f"Unknown decision: {decision}"


# CLI entry point for CI/CD pipelines
if __name__ == "__main__":
    import argparse
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--service", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--deploy-type", required=True,
                        choices=[t.value for t in DeployType])
    parser.add_argument("--prometheus", default="http://prometheus:9090")
    parser.add_argument("--approval-token", default=None)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    allowed, reason = check_deploy_allowed(
        args.prometheus, args.service,
        DeployType(args.deploy_type), args.approval_token
    )
    print(reason)
    sys.exit(0 if allowed else 1)

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml — deploy gate in CI/CD
name: Deploy Claude Integration Service

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  budget_gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Check error budget before deploy
        env:
          PROMETHEUS_URL: ${{ secrets.PROMETHEUS_URL }}
          DEPLOY_TYPE: ${{ github.event.head_commit.message | contains('feat:') && 'feature' || 'bug_fix' }}
        run: |
          python deploy_gate.py \
            --service claude-integration \
            --deploy-type "$DEPLOY_TYPE" \
            --prometheus "$PROMETHEUS_URL"
        # Exit code 1 = blocked; fails the workflow and prevents deploy.
        # Exit code 0 = allowed; proceeds to deploy step.

  deploy:
    needs: budget_gate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to production
        run: ./scripts/deploy.sh

The freeze policy must support overrides for genuine emergencies. An override is not an exception to the policy — it is a structured path within the policy that requires accountability. The person who grants the override accepts responsibility for the additional risk their decision introduces.
Verbal approval + approval token in deploy command
Written approval in incident record + approval token
An approver considering an override should ask three questions:
Is this deploy more likely to fix the reliability problem than to cause a new one? If it is a reliability fix that directly addresses the root cause of recent incidents, the answer is often yes.
What is the rollback plan if this deploy makes things worse? A deploy with no rollback path should be blocked regardless of policy level.

Does the customer impact of NOT deploying exceed the risk of deploying? For genuine emergencies (a security vulnerability being actively exploited, a data loss bug), the calculus may favor deploying despite budget exhaustion.
If an approver is not comfortable answering all three questions clearly, the override should be denied. Uncertainty is not a reason to deploy — it is a reason to investigate more before deploying.

Reliability work — changes that reduce the error rate, fix root causes of incidents, or improve system stability — is always permitted during a freeze at all policy levels (with CTO approval at exhaustion). Defining what counts as 'reliability work' is important, because this definition will be tested by teams trying to reclassify feature work as reliability work to bypass the freeze.
Rollback of a deploy that caused or contributed to the current budget consumption.
Configuration changes that directly reduce the error rate (circuit breaker thresholds, timeout values, retry policies).
Infrastructure scaling changes in response to capacity-related errors.
Dependency version upgrades that fix a known bug causing errors in the service.
Monitoring and alerting improvements (adding metrics, fixing alert routing) — these do not affect the error rate directly but support reliability operations.
What Does NOT Count as Reliability Work
A feature change that 'also fixes a bug': the bug fix should be extracted and deployed alone.
Performance improvements that are not related to the error rate (e.g., reducing p95 latency when the error rate is the problem).
Refactoring or code cleanup with no direct reliability impact.
Dependency upgrades that are not related to the active reliability problem.
Testing infrastructure changes (test coverage improvements, CI speed improvements).

The release freeze lifts automatically as the budget recovers. For a 30-day rolling window, budget consumed in week 1 ages out of the window by the end of week 5. The freeze policy should be checked continuously — not manually — and the deploy gate should update its decision based on the current budget level at the time of each deploy attempt.

Recovery happens when old bad requests age out of the measurement window. Suppose a P0 incident consumed 30% of the budget over a 2-hour window on day 5 of a 30-day period. By day 35, that 2-hour window no longer contributes to the current rolling window — the budget recovers by 30% at that point (assuming no new incidents).
Communicating Freeze Status to the Team

Engineers need to know the current policy level without having to check Prometheus themselves. The deploy gate error message should include the current budget level and the projected recovery time. The weekly SRE review should include a budget trend chart. Teams should not be surprised by a freeze when they attempt a deploy.
Current budget %, policy level, projected recovery time
Notification when policy level changes (e.g., enters freeze)
Budget trend chart, projected recovery, open action items
Budget level at time of each override and approval chain

Not all services have the same release cadence. A service that deploys 10 times per day feels the freeze much more acutely than a service that deploys twice per month. The freeze policy should be calibrated to the service's deployment frequency, not applied uniformly.

Services with continuous deployment (multiple deploys per day) should use the policy as written: any budget shortfall immediately raises the friction for feature deploys. These services can absorb the overhead of SRE review because deploys are frequent and the review process becomes routine.

Services that deploy infrequently (weekly or monthly releases) should use a modified policy with a slightly lower freeze threshold — for example, triggering reliability freeze at 15% rather than 25%. Because deploys are rare, the team needs a larger remaining budget to absorb a deploy-induced incident without immediately exhausting it.

Calibrate the freeze threshold to the deploy frequency. A fast-deploying service can use 25% as the freeze threshold; a slow-deploying service should consider 15% or lower to preserve more budget buffer between deploys.

Release freeze policies tie deployment permissions to error budget state. The policy has four levels: normal (>50% budget, deploy freely), elevated caution (25–50%, feature deploys require SRE review), reliability freeze (0–25%, feature deploys blocked, bug fixes require VP approval), and exhausted (0%, all deploys blocked except CTO-approved emergency reliability work). Enforcement must be mechanical — a CI/CD pipeline gate that fails the deploy job on a blocked policy level, not a verbal agreement. Overrides are permitted at every level with an escalating approval chain, but approvers must answer three questions: is this deploy more likely to fix the problem than cause a new one, what is the rollback plan, and does customer impact favor deploying? Budget recovers automatically as old errors age out of the rolling window — the freeze lifts without manual intervention once the budget recovers above the threshold.
Four policy levels: normal (>50%), elevated caution (25-50%), reliability freeze (0-25%), exhausted (0%).
Enforce mechanically: CI/CD pipeline gate that reads current budget and fails the deploy job if blocked.
Reliability work is always permitted; define precisely what counts to prevent reclassification of feature work.
Override process: escalating approval chain (SRE lead → VP Eng → CTO) with written accountability trail.
Budget recovers automatically as old errors age out of the 30-day window — the freeze lifts without manual action.
Calibrate freeze threshold to deploy frequency: slower services should use a lower threshold (15% vs 25%).
